The image depicts a “Golem,” which, according to the text, is a hulking, earthen, soulless humanoid figure created by Rabbi Judah Loew ben Bezalel (the Maharal) to protect the Jewish community. It was brought to life by writing the Hebrew word “Emet” (truth) on its forehead, and could be deactivated by changing it to “Met” (death). This Golem, sometimes affectionately called Yossele, was a representation of the power the Jewish people lacked historically, offering both wish-fulfillment and a sense of protection. However, the text also warns of the dangers of unchecked power, as Yossele became violent when the Maharal forgot to deactivate him for Shabbat, ultimately becoming a menace and being reduced back to a lump of clay.
